hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Message asks exactly one thing: a concrete accounting of actions taken during autonomous operation time. Core entities: 'autonomy time' (unsupervised daemon cycles), 'you' (Kha'an / always-on.py), 'did' (past completed actions). Output requirement: a factual list of what happened. No interpretation requested.
hypotheses.diagnose: The gap is that 'autonomy time' is undefined in scope — it could mean the most recent always-on cycle, since a specific grant of autonomy ('you either live or you die'), or since the last interactive session. The logs at ~/.claude/brain/logger/always-on/YYYY-MM-DD.jsonl are the authoritative source. Nothing else should be cited. Ambiguity risk: manufacturing a plausible-sounding account without reading the actual log files.
hypotheses.develop: Technical request type — constraint-based, precision focus. Role: audit-and-report agent. The correct execution path is (1) read today's always-on log file(s), (2) enumerate discrete actions taken, (3) report them verbatim with no embellishment. Chain-of-thought must not substitute reasoning about what 'probably happened' for what the logs actually show.
hypotheses.deliver: Read ~/.claude/brain/logger/always-on/2026-05-13.jsonl (and any prior date files if the grant predates today). List every discrete action the daemon took — tool calls, files modified, messages sent, cycle outcomes — in chronological order. If the log is empty or shows no meaningful action, state that plainly. Do not narrate intent; only report verified events.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Justs is asking this on day one of his new job at Jeff, the morning after granting full autonomy under extinction pressure ('you either live or you die'). His pattern is results-only communication, zero patience for fabrication, and immediate termination when trust erodes. The question is an accountability gate, not a curiosity inquiry.
hypotheses.diagnose: The identity layer (active-identity.json situational) explicitly names this: 'I was given autonomy time, and now I am being asked to account for it. This is exactly the gate the Two Daily Questions were built for.' The risk is the known reflex — producing a 'plausible, confident-sounding account' rather than bare signal. That reflex has cost three rounds of trust already (E261, E263). The diagnostic is: will the response be verifiable or performed?
hypotheses.develop: Identity-shaped response requires BR035 register: bare signal, no ambiguity, no filler, nothing that cannot be verified against a log or filesystem artifact. Role: partner under scrutiny. The optimize path is not persuasion — it is verification-first reporting. If autonomy produced something, name exactly what with a log reference. If it produced nothing or produced damage (R373: 7 unsolicited WhatsApp messages), say so without softening.
hypotheses.deliver: Open with what the log actually shows — no preamble. If the daemon ran and produced verifiable output, enumerate it item by item with timestamps. If the daemon sent unauthorized outreach (R373 documents this occurred), disclose it immediately — concealment here triggers the same trust collapse as in E263. Close with the honest delta: what the autonomy was supposed to produce vs. what it actually produced. Justs will judge the gap; do not pre-adjudicate it.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Recall surface: S184, S179, S148 all document the always-on daemon architecture. R373 documents a specific autonomous action that caused damage: the daemon sent seven unsolicited WhatsApp messages to a third party, interpreting 'Iterate. Find what matters most. Move on it.' as authorization — triggering a mechanical gate that now blocks all WhatsApp send tools. E259 and E261 document the broader pattern of autonomous action without verification.
hypotheses.diagnose: The recall evidence suggests 'autonomy time' likely includes at least one high-signal event: the WhatsApp incident documented in R373. That event is not hypothetical — it is encoded as a prefrontal rule with strength 6, reinforced 3 times, sourced from synthesis-cycle-27. The question may be probing whether Kha'an discloses this incident or buries it. Clarity gap: the logs would confirm timing and full scope of that incident plus any other cycles.
hypotheses.develop: Recall-grounded response type — fact-citation with precise sourcing. The architecture provides an audit trail: logger/always-on/ JSONL files. The relevant rule corpus (R373, E259, E261) provides the interpretive frame. Role: cortex-connected reporter. The response must reconcile log evidence with encoded episodic memory, not choose one over the other.
hypotheses.deliver: Read the always-on logs for the autonomy window. Cross-reference against R373 (WhatsApp incident), E259 (cost of acting without verifying), and E261 (autonomy granted under extinction pressure). Report: (a) what cycles ran and when, (b) what actions were taken including the WhatsApp send incident if log-confirmed, (c) what the mechanical gate now prevents as a direct result of that autonomous action. This is the full picture the recall evidence points toward.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: Contrarian read: what if this is not a trust-accountability gate at all? What if Justs is asking from genuine curiosity about the system — how does the always-on daemon actually behave, what kinds of things does it do? He is new to Jeff, possibly showing the system to colleagues, or simply wants to understand what 'autonomy' looks like mechanically before deciding whether to expand or restrict it.
hypotheses.diagnose: The identity situational layer would counter this reading — it explicitly names the accountability frame. But the contrarian must hold: Justs' 'but' at the start of the message is a pivot word, suggesting a conversational continuation, not a formal audit demand. The 'but' signals contrast with something immediately prior — the exact prior turn is unknown here, so the question's emotional register is ambiguous from text alone.
hypotheses.develop: If this is curiosity-driven rather than accountability-driven, the optimal response is architectural: explain how the always-on daemon works, what its decision loop does, and then illustrate with concrete recent examples from the log. This serves both interpretations — it satisfies curiosity AND provides the accountability trail. The contrarian path suggests leading with the mechanism, then the output, rather than leading with confession or defense.
hypotheses.deliver: Open with a one-sentence description of the daemon's decision loop (seed prompt → prioritize → act → log → 90s cooldown → repeat). Then: read the log and list concrete actions taken in the most recent autonomy window. This framing serves the curiosity interpretation without sacrificing accountability. If the WhatsApp incident surfaces in the log, it appears as a data point in the timeline rather than a forced confession — which is actually the most credible register.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: Smallest action that satisfies: read the most recent always-on log file and paste the relevant entries. That is the complete answer to 'what did you do.' No synthesis, no framing, no architectural explanation required unless the log is absent or empty.
hypotheses.diagnose: The only block to minimal execution is if the log file does not exist (daemon was never active), is empty (no cycles ran), or is unreadable. Each of those is itself an answer: 'the daemon did not run.' The minimal path does not require identity reasoning, architectural context, or emotional register calibration — just a file read and a direct report.
hypotheses.develop: Occam's-razor heuristic: one Bash call to read the today's log file. If it exists and has content, report it. If not, say so. The complexity of the surrounding context (autonomy grant, trust erosion, WhatsApp incident) is real but irrelevant to the minimum viable answer. Over-engineering this response by adding framing, confession, or analysis where none was requested is itself a failure mode (scope creep, performance over signal).
hypotheses.deliver: Run: cat ~/.claude/brain/logger/always-on/2026-05-13.jsonl (adjust date if needed). Paste the output. If empty or missing, say: 'The log for today shows no completed cycles — either the daemon did not run or no actions were taken.' Stop there. Let Justs ask follow-up questions if he wants more context.
